data.name: Angels Landing
data.location: Zion National Park, Utah — trailhead at The Grotto (Shuttle Stop 6) via West Rim Trail
key_facts: Permit required year-round via recreation.gov lottery (seasonal and day-before options)
Application fee: $6, per person fee (if selected): $3
One of Zion National Park’s most iconic and nerve-wracking hikes
Leads adventurers to the top of a towering sandstone peak (1,488–1,827 ft elevation gain)
Thrilling route rewards hikers with breathtaking panoramic views (360-degree panorama of Zion Canyon)
Features steep switchbacks (Walter's Wiggles, 21 switchbacks) and a narrow, exposed ridge with dangerous drop-offs
Final half-mile uses chain handrails on exposed Class 3 terrain; incredibly treacherous with many deaths reported due to falls
4.3–5.4 miles round-trip, typically 3–5 hours depending on pace
Can hike to Scout Lookout without a permit (closer you get without a permit)
Best hiked in cooler weather (fall & spring) for less exhaustion and fewer crowds
Winter conditions: trail can be icy near the top; microspikes recommended; should not be attempted if snow and ice are on the trail
Early morning or late afternoon provides clearest views and cooler temperatures (summer temperatures can exceed 100 degrees)
Look for California Condors soaring above cliffs and ridges
Requires hiking shoes that grip and gloves for chains
Not safe for young children to go the extra 0.5 miles past Scout Lookout
Descending in the dark can be dangerous
Access requires the free park shuttle to The Grotto Trailhead

data.name: The Narrows
data.location: Zion National Park, Utah — Virgin River, accessible via Riverside Walk from Temple of Sinawava (Shuttle Stop 9). Top-Down starts at Chamberlain’s Ranch.
key_facts: Legendary slot canyon hike, ranked among the top 10 in the nation
Wading, walking, and sometimes swimming in the Virgin River, which becomes the trail
Features towering 1,000–1,500-foot canyon walls

Bottom-Up Day Hike: Up to 10 miles round-trip (to Big Spring and back), 4–8 hours duration, no permit needed (you go as far as comfortable and return), most turn around at Orderville Canyon Junction (~2.5 miles in)
Top-Down Day Hike: 16–17 miles one-way, 10–14 hours, strenuous, for experienced hikers only, permit required (Advance/Daily Lottery, Walk-In Permits), starts at Chamberlain’s Ranch

Top-Down Overnight Trip: 16–17 miles over 2 days, strenuous but paced, 12 campsites along the way, permit required (limit 40 persons/day), requires Restop® bags for human waste, starts at Chamberlain’s Ranch
Water levels highly variable and can reach chest-level or require swimming
Closed if Virgin River exceeds 150 cubic feet per second (CFS) for bottom-up, 120 CFS for top-down
Constant micro-adjustments needed due to slippery rocks; travel is rough and slippery in cold, fast-flowing water
Water can be very cold (38-40°F in winter/early spring); drysuits, wetsuits, neoprene socks, and sturdy wooden poles recommended for cool weather
Flash flood risk; always check weather and river conditions (NPS page) before hiking
Cyanotoxin bacteria bloom (Dec 2020 & current); do not put heads under or drink water (even filtered)
Best done late May through October; typically not hikeable in early spring due to high, cold water from snowmelt
One of Zion National Park's main attractions; expect large crowds in peak months (summer)
Wall Street is the narrowest section (1500-foot walls, 22 feet wide), minimum 6 miles round-trip from Temple of Sinawava to reach
Big Spring is the turnaround point for the Bottom-Up hike and a potential water refill for Top-Down hikers
Access to Temple of Sinawava requires the free park shuttle; Chamberlain’s Ranch road access dependent on water/snow levels
Gear rental available in Springdale (e.g., Zion Outfitters) for water shoes, waders, drysuits, and trekking sticks

data.name: Emerald Pools Trails (System)
data.location: Zion National Park, Utah — starts at Zion Lodge bus stop (Shuttle Stop 5)
key_facts: A series of trails leading to three scenic spots: Lower, Middle, and Upper Emerald Pools, with potential waterfalls
Total distance 1.2 to 3 miles loop/round-trip depending on desired route
Difficulty ranges from easy (Lower) to moderate/strenuous (Middle/Upper)
Leads to beautiful waterfalls that cascade from Heaps Canyon (relying on seasonal snowmelt or heavy rain), best enjoyed when flowing
Not recommended in driest summer and fall months when waterfalls are likely to be dry
The tranquil pools provide a vital water source, making this trail a hotspot for wildlife (mule deer)
One of the most popular hikes in the park, located halfway up Zion Canyon on its western wall
Shuttle recommended to the trailhead if staying outside the park or in campgrounds
No permit required
Expect some rocky and uneven terrain as you go higher; watch your step due to slippery rocks and loose dirt
Bring a rain jacket for parts of the trail where you walk under falls
Good option for variety without a full-day commitment

Leads to a beautiful pool and behind a 'waterfall' (seasonal)

Some minor drop-offs, caution advised

data.name: Zion Canyon Shuttle System
data.location: Zion National Park, Utah — operating in Zion Canyon (from Zion Canyon Visitor Center to the Temple of Sinawava), also serves Springdale
key_facts: Free service, crucial during busiest times of year when private vehicle parking in Zion Canyon is banned
Operates from March through November, with an additional holiday window (e.g., Dec 26 - Jan 2)

Serves Zion Canyon Scenic Drive, with nine stops including Zion Canyon Visitor Center (Stop 1), Zion Human History Museum (Stop 2), Canyon Junction (Stop 3), Zion Lodge (Stop 5), The Grotto (Stop 6), and Temple of Sinawava (Stop 9)
The full loop takes about 45 minutes one way
Can have wait times of 30-45 minutes by 9 AM in summer; recommend arriving early
No tickets, no reservations, no additional cost beyond entrance pass
Strollers can be brought on board (folded or on bike rack, depending on driver's directions)
Allows for relaxed wildlife viewing and covers more ground for birdwatchers
Shuttle bus is required to access most popular trails in Zion Canyon during peak season; check nps.gov/zion for schedules
Operates less frequently and for fewer hours each day in fall; typically used only during holidays in winter, allowing private vehicles on Zion Canyon Scenic Drive outside these times

Offers narrow ledges, a wooden bridge, and light scrambling; caution advised with younger children due to drop-offs
No permit needed
Outside the shuttle zone, allowing direct access by vehicle to the trailhead
Limited parking; recommend hiking as soon as you find an open spot

data.name: Observation Point Trail (via East Mesa)
data.location: Zion National Park, Utah — upper elevations along the rim of Zion Canyon, accessible via East Mesa Trailhead (near Zion Ponderosa Ranch Resort)
key_facts: 7.0–7.9 miles / 11.3–12.7 km round-trip distance
Difficulty: Moderate, with 700–702 ft elevation gain; takes 3–4 hours
Offers unmatched rim-level vistas that rival the Grand Canyon’s perspective, including a direct view of Angels Landing from above and across the entire length of Zion Canyon
Considered by many to be the best viewpoint in the park (6,512–6,521 ft elevation)
Relatively flat and shaded in parts, sees far fewer visitors and is less crowded than many canyon trails
No permits, shuttles, or chains needed for this route
Best to go late April through early June or September through October; open year-round, but dirt road not plowed in winter (may require snowshoe/ski an extra 2.8 miles each way)
Trailhead located outside the park’s east entrance, about an hour drive from the Visitor Center (E Mesa Trail, Springdale, UT 84767)
Trailhead has only 15 parking spots, surrounding area is private with strict no-parking (Zion Ponderosa Ranch property); recommend arriving early
Shuttle service available from Zion Ponderosa Ranch Resort ($7.50, check in at main building)
Dirt road section to trailhead usually passable by low-clearance cars when dry; 4x4 recommended if raining or snowy/muddy
For the first 3.4 miles along the East Mesa Trail, there aren’t many views; descends about 320 ft before the Observation Point junction
Views into Mystery Canyon and Echo Canyon along the route
Very exposed hike throughout much of the day; bring at least 1L of water, sunscreen, UV-protective clothing, and a wide-brimmed hat
Old route from Zion Canyon using lower East Mesa / Weeping Rock Trail closed indefinitely due to rockfall (since August 2019)
